name: Central Kalimantan
history: Central Kalimantan has been inhabited for centuries by indigenous Dayak peoples who developed sophisticated agricultural and trading systems along its rivers. The region came under Dutch colonial control in the late 19th century as part of the broader colonization of the Dutch East Indies. During the Japanese occupation in World War II, the area experienced significant hardship, and after independence in 1945, Central Kalimantan became an important province for Indonesia's resource extraction and development initiatives. The establishment of Palangkaraya as the provincial capital in 1957 marked a major turning point in the region's modern development.
culturalNotes: Central Kalimantan is primarily home to the Dayak people, whose rich cultural heritage includes intricate wood carving, traditional longhouse architecture, and distinctive musical instruments like the sapé and gong ensembles. The region maintains strong connections to its natural environment through ceremonies tied to agriculture and river life, with communities celebrating harvest festivals and honoring ancestral traditions. The blend of indigenous practices with Islam and Christianity reflects the region's diverse spiritual heritage.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit from June to September for the drier season.
languageNotes: The primary language is Indonesian, but the local Dayak languages, particularly Dayak Ngaju, are widely spoken in communities throughout Central Kalimantan. Learning basic Indonesian greetings like 'Selamat pagi' (good morning) and 'Terima kasih' (thank you) is helpful for visitors, though English is limited outside major towns like Palangkaraya.

places.name: Palangka Raya
places.description: The capital and largest city of Central Kalimantan, situated on the Kahayan River. It serves as the main gateway and base for exploring the region's rainforests, indigenous cultures, and national parks.

activities.text: Tanjung Puting National Park Day Trip
activities.detail: Visit this renowned orangutan rehabilitation center and wildlife sanctuary located about 2 hours from the city, where you can spot orangutans, proboscis monkeys, and hornbills from a river boat.
Take a guided boat journey along the Kahayan River through the city and surrounding rainforest to observe local life, wildlife, and traditional riverside settlements.
Explore this pristine rainforest park north of the city featuring diverse flora, fauna, and indigenous Dayak territories with guided jungle trekking options.
Learn about Central Kalimantan's history, indigenous cultures, and natural heritage through exhibits of traditional artifacts, weapons, and ethnographic displays at this small but informative museum.
Experience authentic Dayak culture by visiting a traditional longhouse settlement in nearby villages where you can observe daily life, crafts, and meet indigenous community members.
Hike through wetland ecosystems and secondary rainforest around this scenic lake to spot migratory birds and experience the region's biodiversity.

food.detail: Freshwater fish (often catfish or snakehead) grilled over charcoal and seasoned with local spices, garlic, and lime, served with sambal and rice.
A savory rice porridge unique to Central Kalimantan featuring shredded chicken, liver, and aromatic broth typically eaten for breakfast.
Sago grubs (beetle larvae) harvested from rotting wood, either grilled or fried until crispy, considered a protein-rich delicacy among local communities.
A traditional hot beverage made with strong black tea, condensed milk, and a raw egg stirred in while hot, creating a creamy drink unique to Banjarese culture.
Rice cakes wrapped in woven palm leaves and served with chicken curry or savory soup, a staple dish especially during celebrations.

culture.detail: Respect local indigenous culture by learning simple Dayak phrases like 'Selamat pagi' (good morning) and showing genuine interest in traditional customs and beliefs.
When visiting longhouses or remote villages, wear long pants and covered shoulders as a sign of respect for indigenous communities and their traditions.
Always ask locals before photographing people, especially in indigenous settlements, as some Dayak communities prefer to maintain privacy around sacred spaces and personal moments.
If possible, time your visit for late May or early June to experience Gawai Dayak, the major harvest festival celebrating indigenous culture with traditional dances, music, and communal feasting.

places.name: Tanjung Puting National Park
places.description: One of Indonesia's most famous orangutan sanctuaries and a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve covering 415,000 hectares of pristine rainforest. Visitors can spot orangutans, proboscis monkeys, and diverse wildlife from river klotok boat tours.

activities.detail: Take traditional wooden riverboats through the rainforest waterways to spot orangutans, proboscis monkeys, and other wildlife in their natural habitat.
Visit the famous research station and orangutan rehabilitation center where you can observe semi-wild orangutans during feeding times.
Explore this visitor center and research outpost located along the Sekonyer River to learn about orangutan conservation and spot wildlife.
Hike through the dense tropical forest on guided trails to experience the park's biodiversity and search for elusive wildlife.
Take evening boat tours to spot nocturnal animals like slow lorises, flying squirrels, and night-active birds along the Sekonyer River.
Seek out the distinctive long-nosed monkeys endemic to Borneo as they gather in riverside trees, especially during early morning and late afternoon.

food.detail: Grilled freshwater fish caught from local rivers, seasoned with traditional spices and served with sambal and rice at riverside lodges.
A hearty rice porridge served with fried shallots, eggs, and savory broth, popular as a filling breakfast among local guides and boatmen.
A tangy soup made with local fish or chicken, tamarind, and aromatic spices that reflects Central Kalimantan's coastal-rainforest cuisine.
Fragrant turmeric rice cooked in coconut milk, commonly served at park lodges as an accompaniment to main dishes.
A traditional protein source of indigenous Dayak people, sometimes offered at local homestays as a cultural delicacy, either grilled or fried.

culture.detail: Maintain at least 7 meters distance from orangutans during boat tours and photography, as they are wild animals and sudden movements can stress them.
Employ knowledgeable local guides from Kumai or Pangkalan Bun who understand animal behavior, park regulations, and contribute directly to conservation efforts.
Respect seasonal access restrictions and official visiting hours to protect wildlife breeding and nesting periods, particularly during monsoon seasons.
Show respect for the indigenous Dayak communities living around the park by asking permission before photographing people and supporting local homestay businesses.

places.name: Kumai
places.description: A small port town serving as the main gateway to Tanjung Puting National Park, located at the mouth of the Sekonyer River. Most travelers stay here briefly before heading into the park for wildlife expeditions.

activities.text: Tanjung Puting National Park boat tours
activities.detail: Multi-day klotok (traditional wooden boat) expeditions departing from Kumai to spot orangutans, proboscis monkeys, and gibbons in their natural habitat.
Evening boat ride along the mangrove-lined river where you can observe wildlife activity and local fishing practices during golden hour.
Early morning exploration of the bustling waterfront market where local fishermen sell fresh catch and you can observe traditional trading practices.
Short guided walks through the village and along the riverbanks to see local life, traditional pile-built houses, and small-scale fishing operations.
Visit the Camp Leakey research station or similar facilities within accessible distance to learn about orangutan rescue and conservation efforts.
Join fishermen on traditional boats to learn their methods and understand the relationship between the port community and the river.

activities.text: Bukit Baka Bukit Raya National Park day trip
activities.detail: Venture inland from Kumai to explore this lesser-visited park featuring forest trails, endemic wildlife, and pristine rainforest ecosystems.

food.detail: Freshly caught river fish grilled over charcoal with lime, chilies, and local spices, reflecting the day's catch from nearby waters.
A comforting rice porridge with shredded fish, traditionally eaten for breakfast and prepared with turmeric and garlic.
River prawns fried with shrimp paste and served with steamed rice, a signature dish showcasing Kumai's fresh seafood.
A fragrant spiced soup from South Kalimantan featuring turmeric, lemongrass, and often served with rice cakes or potatoes.
Mud crabs fried in yellow turmeric paste, a coastal specialty available in local warungs during crab season.

culture.detail: The region is home to Dayak communities, so visitors should ask permission before photographing people and show respect for local spiritual beliefs tied to the forest.
Boats and rivers are the primary transport; wear life jackets when requested by boat operators and understand that river schedules depend on tides and weather.

culture.text: Early morning departures for park tours
culture.detail: Most klotok expeditions depart before sunrise to maximize wildlife sightings, so expect very early wake-up calls if you book multi-day trips.
Kumai has limited ATM access and many local establishments only accept cash in Indonesian Rupiah, so bring sufficient funds before arriving.

places.name: Pangkalan Bun
places.description: A bustling riverside town and administrative center near Tanjung Puting, offering basic amenities and boat connections. It's a practical hub for those organizing expeditions into the national park or exploring the region.

activities.detail: Organize multi-day boat expeditions from Pangkalan Bun to see orangutans, proboscis monkeys, and river wildlife in their natural habitat.
Take a scenic boat ride along the Kumai River, the main waterway connecting Pangkalan Bun to the national park, observing local villages and mangrove forests.
Navigate the tributary rivers by speedboat or traditional longjboat to reach more remote areas and encounter wildlife deeper in the park.
Explore the morning wet market near the town center to see fresh catch from the Kapuas River and local produce while interacting with residents.
Visit Camp Leakey or other rehabilitation facilities within the park to learn about orangutan rescue and conservation efforts directly from local guides.
Stroll through the administrative center to see colonial architecture, government buildings, and get oriented with basic services like banks and pharmacies.

food.detail: Grilled freshwater catfish (patin) from local rivers, typically seasoned with spices and served with sambal and rice.
Boiled freshwater giant prawns caught from the Kapuas and Sekonyer rivers, served with chili dipping sauce and lime.
A spiced Banjarese turmeric soup with beef or chicken, traditionally eaten for breakfast or as a light meal in the region.
Deep-fried snakehead fish (gabus), a local favorite that's crispy outside and tender inside, often served at riverfront warungs.
A sour and spicy fish soup typical of Kalimantan, made with river fish, tamarind, and local herbs, warming and flavorful.

culture.detail: Always follow guide instructions during river travel, wear life jackets, and avoid sudden movements or loud noises to prevent startling wildlife.
When passing Dayak villages or during park visits, ask permission before photographing people and support local guides and boatmen fairly.
The rainy season (October–April) can make roads muddy and boat travel unpredictable, so plan visits during dry season (May–September) for easier access.
Wear modest clothing in Pangkalan Bun's public areas and town markets, as it is a conservative Muslim-majority community in Central Kalimantan.

places.name: Bukit Raya National Park
places.description: A remote mountain national park featuring the region's highest peak (Bukit Raya) and primary dipterocarp forests. It's excellent for trekking and observing endemic wildlife, though it requires more effort to access than Tanjung Puting.

activities.detail: A challenging multi-day trek to Central Kalimantan's highest peak at 2,278 meters, offering panoramic views of primary dipterocarp forest and the surrounding mountain ranges.
Navigate through old-growth rainforest with towering hardwood trees to spot endemic wildlife including sun bears, clouded leopards, and orangutans in their natural habitat.
Join guided early-morning treks to observe endemic birds, gibbons, and other fauna when they are most active, with experienced local guides who know animal behavior patterns.
Follow mountain streams through the forest to discover remote waterfalls and pristine natural pools where you can cool off during strenuous hikes.
Participate in guided walks focusing on medicinal plants, rare orchids, and endemic flora used traditionally by Dayak communities.
Experience nocturnal wildlife including flying insects, nocturnal mammals, and unique sounds of the rainforest under expert guide supervision with flashlights.
Learn about the park's ecology, conservation efforts, and endemic species through displays and information sessions before beginning your trek.

food.detail: Fresh local river fish seasoned with turmeric, garlic, and chili, then grilled over open flame and served with sambal and lime at park lodges.
Protein-rich beetle larvae traditionally harvested from rotting wood, either fried until crispy or cooked in coconut milk, considered a delicacy among Dayak guides.
Rice cooked in coconut milk with turmeric and served with vegetables, a staple meal provided at park accommodations and base camps.
A warming soup made with wild jungle vegetables, bamboo shoots, and local herbs that porters and guides prepare on multi-day treks.
Dried game meat shredded and cooked with spices, traditionally prepared by local communities and sometimes available at base camp meals.

culture.detail: Employ experienced Dayak guides who possess deep knowledge of forest trails, wildlife behavior, and cultural history while supporting local communities economically.
Ask guides about culturally significant areas and avoid disturbing locations important to indigenous communities; some forests hold spiritual significance.
Guides primarily speak Indonesian and local Dayak languages, so learning key phrases like 'terima kasih' (thank you) and 'berapa jauh?' (how far?) enhances communication.
Share meals with guides and porters, as communal eating is culturally important and provides opportunities to learn about their lives and forest knowledge.

places.name: Sampit
places.description: A town in South Kalimantan's border region known for its timber trade and river access, serving as a secondary gateway to the rainforests. It offers a less touristy alternative to Pangkalan Bun with similar access to nature.

activities.detail: Take a guided speedboat or traditional wooden boat along the Kapuas River to spot proboscis monkeys, dolphins, and river wildlife in their natural habitat.

activities.text: Tanjung Puting National Park day trip
activities.detail: Visit the renowned orangutan rehabilitation centers at Camp Leakey or Camp Tanggui, about 2–3 hours from Sampit by river.
Explore Sampit's working timber trade heritage by visiting local sawmills and the active river port to understand the region's logging economy.
Stroll through the bustling wet market early in the morning to see local produce, fish, and traditional goods sold by Dayak and Banjarese traders.
Tour a working crocodile breeding facility that conserves saltwater crocodiles and educates visitors about local reptile species.
Paddle a traditional canoe through blackwater rivers and oxbow lakes into pristine peat swamp forests to observe endemic birds and reptiles.

food.detail: Freshly caught river fish from the Kapuas, grilled over charcoal and seasoned with chili, lime, and local spices, served at riverfront warungs.
A savory rice porridge cooked with coconut milk and served with salted fish, fried shallots, and sambal, a Banjarese comfort food staple.
Snakehead fish, a local freshwater specialty, deep-fried until crispy and served with sambal matah or sweet soy sauce.
A warming turmeric-based broth with chicken or beef, potatoes, and hard-boiled eggs, reflecting Banjarese culinary traditions.
River crabs cooked in a creamy coconut gravy with ginger and garlic, a local delicacy found in family-run seafood restaurants.

culture.detail: Always ask permission and hire licensed local guides for river activities; the Kapuas is sacred to indigenous Dayak communities and requires cultural sensitivity.
Visit during the dry season (June–September) when river levels are stable and wildlife is more visible; wet season floods can disrupt transport and activities.
Accept tea or coffee offered by locals as a sign of respect, and always greet elders with a slight nod or handshake; direct eye contact shows sincerity.
Be mindful of Sampit's timber industry heritage and avoid discussing deforestation insensitively; support eco-tourism operators to promote sustainable practices.

places.name: Sebangau National Park
places.description: A lesser-known peatland national park near Palangka Raya protecting one of the world's largest orangutan populations and unique peat swamp forests. It offers a more adventurous and less-visited alternative for wildlife viewing than Tanjung Puting.

activities.detail: Paddle through peat swamp waterways early in the morning to observe wild Bornean orangutans feeding in their natural habitat, guided by experienced local trackers.
Tour the Orangutan Foundation's field research station to learn about peatland conservation, orangutan behavior studies, and ongoing habitat protection efforts.
Hike through unique lowland peat forest ecosystems to observe proboscis monkeys, gibbons, and endemic plant species while learning about carbon sequestration.
Join guided night canoe expeditions to spot nocturnal species including slow lorises, flying squirrels, and various owl species illuminated by spotlights.
Explore designated birding routes to identify over 200 species including the rare Storm's Stork, Flat-headed Eagle, and multiple hornbill species.
Take a longer boat expedition upriver to reach remote forest sections with higher concentrations of wildlife and pristine peat swamp landscapes.
Participate in guided educational sessions about peatland hydrology, carbon storage, and the ecological importance of Indonesia's peat ecosystems.

food.detail: Freshwater fish caught from local rivers, grilled over charcoal and served with sambal and lime, a staple in nearby Palangka Raya restaurants.
A rich Banjarese turmeric-based soup with beef or chicken, potatoes, and aromatic spices, representing the culinary heritage of Central Kalimantan.
A savory rice porridge cooked in coconut milk and broth, traditionally eaten for breakfast or as a light meal by local communities.
A traditional Central Kalimantan fish curry with turmeric, galangal, and other spices, reflecting the region's indigenous cooking methods.
Seasonal local fruits including rambutan, mangosteen, and durian purchased at Palangka Raya markets, offering authentic tropical flavors.

culture.detail: Always engage certified guides from the community who understand orangutan behavior, forest safety, and weather patterns essential for navigating peat swamps.
Maintain at least 7 meters from wild orangutans, speak softly, and avoid sudden movements to prevent distressing the animals and ensure their natural feeding patterns continue undisturbed.
Choose operators partnered with the Orangutan Foundation or similar organizations where entrance fees directly fund habitat protection and local employment.

The park involves muddy trails, insects, and unpredictable weather; locals expect visitors to embrace adventure rather than seeking luxury, so bring proper hiking gear and maintain flexible expectations.

places.name: Palangka Raya Market District
places.description: The vibrant market area in Palangka Raya where locals trade tropical fruits, handicrafts, and traditional goods from across Central Kalimantan. It provides insight into daily life and indigenous cultures of the region.

activities.detail: Explore stalls overflowing with local fruits like rambutan, mangosteen, durian, and calamansi that reflect Central Kalimantan's agricultural bounty.
Purchase authentic beaded jewelry, woven baskets, and carved wooden items directly from Dayak artisans displaying their traditional craftsmanship.

activities.text: Visit the Fish and Seafood Section
activities.detail: Wander through the riverside market's aquatic goods area where freshly caught fish, river shrimp, and local seafood are sold daily.
Arrive before 8 AM to witness the energetic early trading when locals gather to shop for daily necessities and farmers bring fresh produce.
Capture candid moments of vendors, shoppers, and the colorful arrangement of goods that tell stories of daily life in Central Kalimantan.
Browse traditional batik fabrics and locally-made textiles alongside modern clothing vendors in the bustling textile section.

food.detail: A aromatic Banjarese turmeric-based soup with beef or chicken, potatoes, and local herbs that represents Central Kalimantan's signature comfort food.
Creamy rice congee served with shredded chicken, fried shallots, and a squeeze of lime, commonly eaten as a market breakfast.
Fresh river fish from local catches grilled over charcoal and seasoned with sambal, garlic, and lemongrass, available at market food stalls.
Compressed rice cakes wrapped in woven palm leaves, often sold with peanut sauce or served alongside grilled meats at market vendors.
Fried stuffed crepes filled with minced meat, eggs, and onions, a popular sweet or savory snack sold by street vendors throughout the market.

culture.detail: Haggling is expected in the market, but remain polite and friendly; starting at 70-80% of the asking price is typical for non-fixed goods.
Always ask vendors before photographing their stalls or goods, as some may feel uncomfortable or request a small payment for images.
Wear loose, breathable clothing and avoid revealing attire to show respect to local customs; bring a scarf to cover shoulders if needed.
Using polite phrases like 'Selamat pagi' (good morning) and 'Terima kasih' (thank you) builds goodwill with vendors and enhances your market experience.

places.name: Kahayan River
places.description: A major tributary flowing through pristine rainforest and home to thriving orangutan and wildlife populations in its upper reaches. Boat expeditions along the river offer excellent opportunities for nature photography and wildlife encounters.

activities.detail: Navigate the upper reaches of Kahayan River by boat to observe wild orangutans in their natural rainforest habitat, particularly around the tributary areas near Tanjung Puting National Park.
Capture the dense primary and secondary rainforest lining the river banks with guided photo expeditions that highlight endemic bird species, proboscis monkeys, and lush vegetation.
Undertake a 3–5 day boat journey along the Kahayan from Palangkaraya downstream, camping at riverside settlements and conducting early morning and dusk wildlife observation sessions.
Stop at traditional Dayak longhouse communities along the river to learn about indigenous culture, handicrafts, and traditional hunting and fishing practices.
Search for Brahminy kites, white-bellied sea eagles, and various hornbill species during boat rides, especially in early morning hours when bird activity peaks.
Navigate shallow tributaries and peat swamp areas to observe unique wetland ecosystems, including rare plant species and specialized wildlife adapted to acidic water conditions.

food.detail: Fresh freshwater fish caught from the Kahayan, seasoned with local spices and grilled over charcoal, served with sambal and rice at riverside lodges and villages.
Protein-rich beetle larvae harvested from rotting sago palms, traditionally eaten raw or lightly cooked by Dayak communities as a delicacy along the river.
Young bamboo shoots foraged from riverside vegetation, cooked in coconut milk with turmeric and chilies, reflecting Dayak culinary traditions.
Local freshwater catfish simmered in a light broth with galangal, lemongrass, and river herbs, a staple comfort food in riverside settlements.
Rice porridge infused with local river fish, ginger, and garlic, commonly eaten for breakfast at guesthouses and small warung along the Kahayan.

culture.text: Respect Local Beliefs and Sacred Sites
culture.detail: Many areas along the Kahayan hold spiritual significance for Dayak communities; ask permission before photographing people or entering villages, and avoid disturbing ritual spaces.

culture.text: Learn Basic Indonesian and Dayak Greetings
culture.detail: While English is limited in remote areas, learning phrases like 'Selamat pagi' (good morning) and 'Terima kasih' (thank you) shows respect and enhances interactions with local guides and villagers.
Wear long pants and covered shoulders when visiting Dayak longhouses or villages as a sign of respect for local customs, even though the climate is hot and humid.
Accept invitations to share meals with local families when offered, as food-sharing is a central part of Dayak hospitality and relationship-building.

places.name: Muara Teweh
places.description: An upriver trading town on the Kahayan River serving Dayak communities and agriculture, accessible by boat from Palangka Raya. It provides an authentic look at inland Central Kalimantan life and traditional river settlements.

activities.detail: Take a motorized canoe upstream to observe traditional Dayak longhouses, floating gardens, and riverside villages nestled along the river.
Explore Muara Teweh's river market where Dayak traders sell fresh produce, fish, handicrafts, and forest products in a lively morning setting.
Tour working smallholder farms on the outskirts where local families cultivate rubber and cocoa, key agricultural products of the region.
Arrange meetings with Dayak community leaders to learn about traditional practices, handicrafts, and daily life in authentic inland settlements.
Hire local guides for short treks into surrounding secondary forest to spot wildlife, medicinal plants, and gain insight into traditional ecological knowledge.
Position yourself along the riverbank at dawn to capture mist-covered waters, traditional wooden structures, and local fishing activities.

food.detail: Fresh Kahayan River fish wrapped in banana leaves and grilled over charcoal, seasoned simply with salt and local spices.
A savory rice porridge topped with shredded dried fish, boiled egg, and served with fried shallots and sambal, common as breakfast or light meal.
Protein-rich larvae found in rotting sago palms, traditionally roasted or fried and considered a delicacy among Dayak communities.
A hearty local stew combining river vegetables, cassava leaves, and sometimes fish or shrimp, flavored with coconut milk and spices.
River fish simmered in a fragrant broth of turmeric, ginger, galangal, and local herbs, reflecting Central Kalimantan's preference for aromatic flavors.

culture.detail: The Kahayan River is central to daily life; avoid loud behavior near water and always ask permission before photographing boats, nets, or fishing activities.
Dayak culture values respect for age and authority, so acknowledge the oldest person in any gathering and use polite Indonesian or learn basic Dayak greetings.
Although inland, modest clothing—covering shoulders and knees—is appreciated in this traditional community and shows cultural respect.
While some residents speak English, many do not; learning simple Indonesian phrases greatly enhances interaction and demonstrates genuine interest in the community.

places.name: Central Kalimantan Museum
places.description: The main cultural museum in Palangka Raya showcasing Dayak artifacts, traditional weapons, clothing, and ethnographic exhibits. It's an essential stop for understanding the indigenous heritage and history of Central Kalimantan.

activities.detail: Examine traditional blowpipes (sumpit), parangs, and shield displays that represent the hunting and warrior heritage of Dayak communities.
View intricate handwoven fabrics including traditional songket patterns and beaded clothing that showcase Dayak artistic techniques passed down through generations.
View ethnographic displays and photographs depicting traditional Dayak longhouse (rumah panjang) architecture and communal living arrangements.
Observe ceremonial masks, ornamental skulls, and shamanic artifacts that illustrate the spiritual practices and belief systems of Central Kalimantan's indigenous peoples.

activities.text: Review Colonial and Modern History Exhibits
activities.detail: Browse displays documenting Central Kalimantan's transition from pre-colonial times through Dutch rule to modern Indonesian statehood.
Capture images of the museum building's design, which itself incorporates elements inspired by traditional Dayak architectural styles.

food.detail: A savory rice porridge cooked with turmeric and local vegetables, often served for breakfast and representing comfort food across Central Kalimantan.
A sour and spicy fish soup made with tamarind, local freshwater fish, and aromatic herbs that is a signature dish of the region.
Fried spring rolls filled with bamboo shoots and minced meat, reflecting both indigenous and Chinese culinary influences in Central Kalimantan.
A regional variant of Banjarese soup featuring local meats or fish with turmeric and spices, adapted to Central Kalimantan ingredients.
Local bananas fried until golden and crispy, often sold by street vendors near the museum as a popular snack or dessert.

culture.detail: Handle and photograph ritual artifacts with reverence, as some items on display hold spiritual significance and asking staff about their cultural context shows respect.
Plan your visit around Gawai Dayak festival (late May/June) or other indigenous celebrations when the museum may host special exhibitions and cultural performances.
Staff members are often knowledgeable about Dayak heritage and can provide context that exhibits alone cannot convey, so conversations enhance understanding.
Central Kalimantan is home to multiple Dayak subgroups with distinct traditions, and the museum reflects this diversity, so avoid treating all exhibits as monolithic.

restaurants.name: Warung Soto Banjar Cempaka
restaurants.description: Humble warung serving authentic soto banjar, a traditional South Kalimantan soup with rich spice blends.

restaurants.name: Rumah Makan Kalimantan
restaurants.description: Family-run restaurant showcasing Central Kalimantan regional dishes with freshwater fish specialties.

restaurants.name: Cafe Mahakam
restaurants.description: Modern cafe with local and Indonesian comfort food, popular with both tourists and locals for breakfast.
restaurants.mustOrder: Nasi kuning with sambal and ikan asin

restaurants.name: Warung Nasi Goreng Bapak Hendra
restaurants.description: Simple street-level warung perfecting fried rice with smoky wok flavor and fresh local ingredients.
restaurants.mustOrder: Nasi goreng kampung with telur mata sapi

restaurants.name: Rumah Makan Samarinda
restaurants.description: Established restaurant in Sampit offering traditional East Kalimantan dishes adapted for Central Kalimantan's palate.

restaurants.name: Soto Ayam Cak Somat
restaurants.description: Long-standing stall near Kumai port famous for silky turmeric chicken soup with aromatic spice paste.

restaurants.name: Floating Restaurant Sungai
restaurants.description: Unique riverside floating platform restaurant serving fresh catch from Kahayan River with river views.

restaurants.name: Warung Baso Khas Banua
restaurants.description: Casual meatball soup warung with homemade broth and served with fresh rice noodles.

restaurants.name: Restoran Banua Mas
restaurants.description: Upscale Central Kalimantan restaurant highlighting regional cuisine with refined presentation.
restaurants.mustOrder: Garang asem ikan (sour fish soup with traditional spices)

restaurants.name: Warung Kopi Lokal
restaurants.description: Neighborhood coffee warung serving strong local kopi jamu with light snacks.

restaurants.name: Restoran Tanjung Harapan
restaurants.description: Seaside restaurant in Pangkalan Bun with fresh seafood daily catch and traditional coastal preparations.

restaurants.name: Warung Mie Goreng Pak Bambang
restaurants.description: Popular stall known for smoky fried noodles with bold garlic and chili flavors.

restaurants.name: Rumah Makan Tikar Kuning
restaurants.description: Traditional timber-built restaurant serving Sampit's signature dishes including river fish specialties.
restaurants.mustOrder: Ikan bakar Sampit dengan bumbu kuning

restaurants.name: Cafe Buana Kalimantan
restaurants.description: Contemporary cafe blending modern aesthetics with authentic Central Kalimantan recipes.
